\maketitle

\tableofcontents


\chapter*{Executive Description}
\addcontentsline{toc}{chapter}{Executive Description}

The Right-to-Life Deficit Index (RTLDI) quantifies the annual economic cost, in lost GDP, that results when states fail to provide equal and effective protection of the right to life. It translates the nine binary indicators of Right-to-Life Protection (RTLP) into a 0–1 score (R). The core equation, $\Delta G = 0.05 \times (1 - R) \times G_0$, yields the per-capita GDP loss; multiplied by population it produces the aggregate national deficit.

This 2026 Atlas applies the framework to all 193 UN Member States. V-Dem data (latest 2024) supplies the eight governance and civil-liberties components; World Bank data supplies the most recent published GDP per capita as the dynamic baseline ($G_0$) for 2026. The result is a transparent, reproducible map of where incomplete protection of life imposes the largest measurable drag on human economic activity.

The Atlas is intended as a practical reference for NGOs, national human-rights institutions, development agencies, researchers, and—most importantly—for the people and policymakers inside each of the 193 nations who want to understand the concrete economic costs of incomplete right-to-life protection and to use that knowledge as a diagnostic for reform.

A dedicated Diagnostic Guide explains how to read the R scores and 9-component breakdowns as levers.


\chapter{Methodology and Data Sources}

\section{RTLP Score (R)}
R is the simple average of nine binary indicators (0 = no protection, 1 = full protection). Eight indicators are derived from V-Dem v15 (2024 data) using the following thresholds (higher = stronger protection):

\begin{enumerate}
  \item Legal Protections — \texttt{v2cltrnslw} $\ge 2.0$
  \item Independent Judiciary — average(\texttt{v2juhcind}, \texttt{v2juncind}) $\ge 2.0$
  \item Law Enforcement Accountability — \texttt{v2clkill} $\ge 2.0$
  \item Protection Against Arbitrary Detention — \texttt{v2xcl\_acjst} $\ge 0.5$
  \item Freedom from Torture — \texttt{v2cltort} $\ge 2.0$
  \item Civilian Protection in Conflict — \texttt{v2clkill} $\ge 2.0$
  \item Access to Justice — \texttt{v2xcl\_acjst} $\ge 0.5$
  \item Freedom of Expression \& Whistleblower Protections — \texttt{v2x\_freexp} $\ge 0.5$
\end{enumerate}

9. Socioeconomic Conditions — World Bank: undernourishment $\le 5\%$ AND poverty headcount (\$2.15) $\le 10\%$.

R = (number of `Yes' indicators) / 9

\section{Economic Projection}
$\Delta G$ (per-capita loss) = $0.05 \times (1 - R) \times G_0$, where $G_0$ is the most recent published GDP per capita (World Bank, labeled 2026 baseline in this edition). Total national loss = $\Delta G \times$ population.

\section{Data Vintage Note for 2026 Edition}
V-Dem components reflect the latest available year in the source file (2024). GDP per capita ($G_0$) uses the freshest published values available at the time of atlas production. This follows the principle that RTLP governance scores are relatively stable year-to-year while GDP is the more dynamic variable in the equation.

Full crosswalk and binarization rules: \texttt{docs/indicator\_crosswalk.md}

Source equations: Sid J.A. Hubbard, \textit{Causality and Attraction} v3 (2026), DOI 10.5281/zenodo.19468550.

\chapter*{Cartographic Approach and the Nested Map}
\addcontentsline{toc}{chapter}{Cartographic Approach and the Nested Map}

The source document, \textit{Causality and Attraction: A Continuum of Steady States} (Hubbard 2026V3), devotes significant attention to the problem of representation. It critiques the ``insufficiency of flat maps,'' noting that conventional projections like Mercator introduce severe distortions that privilege certain regions while diminishing others. It explicitly praises the ``butterfly map inspired by Buckminster Fuller---a beautiful map, genuinely beautiful---with remarkably low distortion and something approaching exact scale across its lobes.'' The text frames map-making not as neutral technique but as a structural act that must honor the nested causal enclosures it seeks to describe.

The RTLDI ATLAS takes this seriously. The 9 RTLP indicators and the core equation model causal costs within a global human enclosure. The visual representations of those costs must therefore strive for fidelity rather than convenience.

\section*{Hybrid Model for Accuracy and Wholeness}

For all programmatically generated choropleths---the global map, the 22 regional overviews, and the 193 per-nation focus maps---the atlas uses the \textbf{Mollweide} projection (an equal-area pseudocylindrical projection). This choice is made for three reasons that themselves reflect the source framework:

\begin{itemize}
  \item Equal-area preservation ensures that visual weight corresponds to geographic reality, avoiding the kind of arbitrary distortion the book criticizes.
  \item It is fully supported in the lightweight, reproducible tool chain (Plotly + kaleido) used for all map generation.
  \item Any user who clones the repository and supplies their own V-Dem and World Bank data can regenerate every map with standard Python packages, without specialized GIS software or custom projection mathematics. This accessibility is itself an expression of structural integrity and non-interruption.
\end{itemize}

Mollweide is the practical, honest default for the dynamic content of the atlas.

\section*{The Canonical Whole-Earth View}

To more directly embody the source document's appreciation for low-distortion, structurally faithful representations (and its integration of Fuller-inspired synergetic geometry), the atlas presents the following global choropleth as its primary ``whole earth'' anchor. This image is the canonical visual expression of the global enclosure of human rights and economic outcomes that the RTLDI measures.


\begin{center}
  \includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{figures/global_choropleth.png}
\end{center}
\textit{Primary visual anchor for the RTLDI ATLAS. Low-distortion equal-area presentation of enclosure strength across all 193 UN Member States (Mollweide projection). This image, together with the regional and per-nation views, enacts the project's commitment to accuracy and structural wholeness in its execution.}

Users who wish to explore the same data in AuthaGraph, Dymaxion, Goode's interrupted, or other advanced projections are encouraged to do so. The master datasets (CSV and XLSX in \texttt{outputs/atlas/}) contain the necessary ISO3 codes and R values and are designed to be imported directly into professional GIS tools.

See also: Chapter 2 ``The Insufficiency of Flat Maps'' and the Epilogue ``The Nested Map – From Vector to Self'' in Sid J.A. Hubbard, \textit{Causality and Attraction} v3 (2026), DOI 10.5281/zenodo.19468550.

The per-nation and regional choropleths throughout this atlas use the same Mollweide base for consistency and full reproducibility.

182 & Norway & Northern Europe & 1.00 & $0 & $0 m & 5.6 m \\
183 & Denmark & Northern Europe & 1.00 & $0 & $0 m & 6.0 m \\
184 & Latvia & Northern Europe & 1.00 & $0 & $0 m & 1.9 m \\
185 & Ireland & Northern Europe & 1.00 & $0 & $0 m & 5.4 m \\
186 & Luxembourg & Western Europe & 1.00 & $0 & $0 m & 0.7 m \\
187 & Uruguay & South America & 1.00 & $0 & $0 m & 3.4 m \\
188 & Cuba & Caribbean & 0.00 & N/A & N/A & 11.0 m \\
189 & Eritrea & Eastern Africa & 0.00 & N/A & N/A & 3.5 m \\
190 & North Korea & Eastern Asia & 0.00 & N/A & N/A & 26.5 m \\
191 & South Sudan & Eastern Africa & 0.00 & N/A & N/A & 11.9 m \\
192 & Syria & Western Asia & 0.00 & N/A & N/A & 24.7 m \\
193 & Yemen & Western Asia & 0.00 & N/A & N/A & 40.6 m \\

\bottomrule
\end{longtable}

\chapter{UN Regional Summaries}

The following pages (in a complete generation) provide one page per UN region with:
\begin{itemize}
  \item Regional choropleth (Mollweide, same scale).
  \item Aggregates (n countries, total pop, weighted R, total lost GDP).
  \item \textbf{The nations table} with columns for RTLP (R), GDP (G0 per capita), Population, and RTLD I (total lost USD).
  \item Bottom row: REGIONAL TOTAL (weighted R, blank for G0, sum pop, sum lost).
  \item 9-indicator breakdown with \% Yes and attributable lost GDP.
\end{itemize}

Example for Western Africa (high-impact region; real generator populates from the 2026 CSV exactly as the previous fpdf version):

\section*{Western Africa Region}

\begin{center}
  \textit{Regional choropleth here (generated in outputs/figures/regional\_choropleths/western\_africa.png)}
\end{center}

\textbf{Key aggregates (example numbers from data):} 16 countries | Population: XXX million | Pop-weighted Avg R: 0.35 | Total Annual Lost GDP: \$YY billion.

\textbf{Member Nations (RTLP, GDP, Population, RTLD I) with regional totals:}

\begin{longtable}{@{}l c r r r@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{Country} & \textbf{R (RTLP)} & \textbf{G0 (\$)} & \textbf{Population} & \textbf{Lost (USD)} \\
\midrule
\endfirsthead
\toprule
\textbf{Country} & \textbf{R (RTLP)} & \textbf{G0 (\$)} & \textbf{Population} & \textbf{Lost (USD)} \\
\midrule
\endhead
\bottomrule
\endfoot
Nigeria & 0.33 & 2,162 & 232.7 m & \$8.4 bn \\
Ghana & 0.56 & 2,176 & 34.1 m & \$0.9 bn \\
... (full list of 16 from the 2026 atlas CSV, sorted by lost or alpha) \\
\bottomrule
\textbf{REGIONAL TOTAL} & \textbf{0.35 (wtd avg)} & -- & \textbf{XXX.X m} & \textbf{\$YY.Y bn} \\
\bottomrule
\end{longtable}

(The generator fills the exact rows and computes the weighted R + sums for the totals row using the same logic as the global summary and the prior fpdf regional tables.)

The 9 RTLP indicator breakdown for the region (prevalence of Yes + attributable lost GDP) follows immediately, using the aggregated components from the breakdown JSON.

\chapter{Detailed Nation Profiles (Alphabetical)}

Each profile is a self-contained page (or short spread) with refined LaTeX layout:
\begin{itemize}
  \item Clear header with country, ISO3, UN region, R (fraction of 9), data vintage.
  \item Economic impact as \texttt{tcolorbox} (colored frame, matching prior design).
  \item Two images side-by-side or stacked: 3-year trend plot + regional zoom choropleth (both from the Mollweide series generated by the map scripts).
  \item 9 RTLP indicators as a compact description list or tabular, with color-coded Yes/No, raw values, and short descriptions.
\end{itemize}

Example stub (the generator produces the real content for all 193):

\section*{Nigeria (NGA)}

\textit{UN Region: Western Africa | RTLP R = 0.33 (3/9) | V-Dem year: 2024 | G0 year: 2026}

\begin{economicbox}
G0 (GDP per capita): \$2,162 \quad | \quad Per-capita annual loss ($\Delta G$): \$36 \\
Total annual GDP loss: \$8.4 billion \quad | \quad Population: 232.7 million
\end{economicbox}

\textit{(Here: \includegraphics for the 3-year trend PNG and the regional zoom PNG, sized to fit nicely.)}

\textbf{RTLP Score Breakdown — 9 Indicators}

\begin{enumerate}
  \item Existence of Legal Protections \no{No} — raw: -0.95 (thresh 2.0). Provisions explicitly protecting the right to life.
  \item Independent Judiciary \no{No} — raw: -2.43. Independent judiciary capable of upholding right-to-life laws.
  \item ... (full 9 from the breakdown JSON for this country)
\end{enumerate}

See the fpdf generator (and the aggregation already present) for the exact per-country data. The LaTeX version refines the visual design (typography, boxes, table rules, image placement, consistent colors from the source framework) while keeping 100\% fidelity to the 2026 data rule, the 9 verbatim indicators, the $\Delta G$ equation, and the hybrid cartographic model.

\chapter*{Data Attribution and Sources}
\addcontentsline{toc}{chapter}{Data Attribution and Sources}

Primary Data
\begin{itemize}
  \item V-Dem (Varieties of Democracy) Country-Year Full+Others, version 15 (2024 data release).
  \item World Bank World Development Indicators (WDI) via API — GDP per capita (current US\$), population, prevalence of undernourishment, and poverty headcount at \$2.15 (2017 PPP).
  \item United Nations Member States list (193 sovereign states).
\end{itemize}

Source Framework
The RTLDI equation, the nine RTLP indicators, and the nested causal interpretation are taken from:
Sid J.A. Hubbard, \textit{Causality and Attraction: A Continuum of Steady States} (Version 3, May 2026), DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.19468550.

\chapter*{Credits and Acknowledgments}
This work is released in the spirit of the original document: to map, with transparent data and low-distortion representation, the economic costs of incomplete protection of the right to life.

The hybrid cartographic model (Mollweide for generated views + canonical low-distortion anchor) and the LaTeX refinement are deliberate choices to demonstrate commitment to accuracy and structural wholeness.

\end{document}
